evidence: Package topology maps cleanly to domain capabilities: api, config, exploration, model, query, indexer, utils — each owns one concern and the boundaries are non-overlapping.

Handler decomposition in the api package is consistent: SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers each own one API facet; WordSketchApiServer is strictly a router.

config package demonstrates single-responsibility at the file level: GrammarConfig (data), GrammarConfigLoader (file I/O + parsing), GrammarConfigSerializer (JSON output) — three distinct concerns in three files.
Package-info files exist for every package and contain accurate, useful orientation text — README/structure alignment is trustworthy.

model.exploration types (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate) are named after adjectives despite the exploration system supporting four grammatical relations (adj_predicate, adj_modifier, subject_of, object_of) — the model vocabulary does not match the stated generality of the feature.

SemanticFieldExplorer is a concrete class, not an interface; QueryExecutor is correctly abstracted but the exploration layer's primary entry point is not, creating an asymmetry at the api/exploration boundary.

WordSketchApiServer registers /api/semantic-field as a deprecated alias that logs a warning on every server startup and on every invocation — dead-weight surface that inflates the public API contract and adds startup noise.

ExplorationHandlers three handler methods have structurally different parameter flows: handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti share resolveRelationConfig, while handleSemanticFieldComparison is cross-relational and does not — this asymmetry is documented but adds conceptual overhead for a developer adding a fourth handler.

strengths: Package topology is domain-aligned and non-overlapping: api, config, exploration, model, query, indexer, utils each own one domain concern without overlap.
Handler decomposition in the api package is consistent and predictable — a new engineer can explain why each handler file exists.
The config package cleanly separates data (GrammarConfig), loading (GrammarConfigLoader), and serialization (GrammarConfigSerializer) into dedicated files.
Every package has a package-info.java with accurate orientation text; the model package even documents the DTO vs. model-with-derived-accessors split.
Main.java is a thin CLI router that delegates immediately to typed handlers — entry point surface is minimal and predictable.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are localized misalignments rather than structural ownership failures: the model layer uses adjective-specific names for what is architecturally a generic collocate-profile system, one concrete class violates the abstraction pattern established elsewhere, and a deprecated endpoint inflates the API surface without removal.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits comfortably above the 'solid but uneven' range because its top-level decomposition is genuinely clean: packages map to domain capabilities, the handler split is predictable, and config concerns are well-separated. The score is pulled down from the high-80s by two cross-cutting issues: the exploration model layer names types after adjectives (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult.allAdjectives) in a system that explicitly supports non-adjective relations, creating a vocabulary mismatch between config and model; and SemanticFieldExplorer is a concrete class where QueryExecutor is an interface, breaking the abstraction pattern. Neither issue is structural enough to drop below 75, but together they reflect a partial gap between the codebase's stated generality and its model-layer ownership.

issues.summary: Exploration model layer uses adjective-specific names (AdjectiveProfile, allAdjectives) in a system that supports four grammatical relations
evidence: AdjectiveProfile.java is the result type for compareCollocateProfiles, but the system supports subject_of and object_of relations that return verbs, not adjectives.

ComparisonResult.allAdjectives() and fullyShared()/partiallyShared()/specific() return List<AdjectiveProfile> — the word 'adjective' appears throughout a type that conceptually holds any collocate profile.

The config layer uses the neutral term RelationType (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) and RelationConfig.collocatePosGroup(), but the model layer that stores results uses adjective vocabulary regardless of which relation was used.
issues.suggestion: Rename AdjectiveProfile to CollocateProfile (and its field/method references accordingly), and rename ComparisonResult.allAdjectives() to allCollocates(). This aligns the model vocabulary with the config vocabulary and removes the false constraint that comparison is adjective-only.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer is a concrete class where QueryExecutor is an interface, creating an inconsistent abstraction boundary at the api/exploration boundary
evidence: ExplorationHandlers accepts SemanticFieldExplorer as a concrete type parameter — there is no SemanticFieldExplorer interface.
WordSketchApiServer has a convenience constructor that calls new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor, grammarConfig) directly.
QueryExecutor is an interface and BlackLabQueryExecutor is its implementation — the query layer correctly establishes the abstraction, but the exploration layer does not mirror it.
issues.suggestion: Extract a SemanticFieldExplorer interface (methods: exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles, fetchExamples), rename the current class to DefaultSemanticFieldExplorer or BlackLabSemanticFieldExplorer, and update ExplorationHandlers and WordSketchApiServer to depend on the interface. This makes the abstraction pattern consistent across both major boundary layers.

issues.summary: Deprecated /api/semantic-field alias remains on the public API surface, logging startup warnings and polluting the route registry
evidence: WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes() calls logger.warn at startup: 'Registering deprecated endpoint /api/semantic-field — use /api/semantic-field/compare instead. Will be removed in v2.0.'
The handler also logs a warn on every individual request to the deprecated path.
The endpoint is documented in the class Javadoc alongside the canonical endpoints, making the API surface appear larger than it is.
issues.suggestion: Remove the /api/semantic-field alias route and its registration entirely. If backward compatibility is required for a specific release window, return HTTP 301/308 to /api/semantic-field/compare rather than registering a full handler — this makes the intent explicit without polluting the runtime log or the documented API surface.

root_causes: The exploration feature was likely designed initially for adjective-only relations and then generalised to support verb relations without updating the model layer naming.

The abstraction inconsistency between QueryExecutor (interface) and SemanticFieldExplorer (class) suggests the exploration layer was added after the query layer and did not inherit the same interface discipline.
The deprecated endpoint reflects a common pattern of deferred cleanup — the canonical path exists and is documented, but the old path was never removed.
